Often we  can make an uncountable word countable by putting a piece of or a 1 like travel, but it's often tiring. Did you have a good journey?

The  following rules will help, but to know exactly how  a (countable) and glass (uncountable); vegetables (countable) and fruit clear: compare a journey (countable) and travel (uncountable); a glass house is normally a countable noun, and water is not. But it is not always so Usually it is easy to see whether a noun is countable or uncountable. Obviously 2  problems grammar *119   countable and uncountable nouns: basic information There are times when I just want to stop work.               -> Have a good time.

- Don't hurry - there's plenty of time.

corresponding to more ‘ general’  and more ‘ particular’  meanings. Compare: Many abstract nouns can have both uncountable and countable uses, often 6 abstract      nouns:    time,     life,    experience,           etc (e.g. a grain of corn, a blade of grass), ►     121.

For words that are used to talk about one ‘ piece’  of uncountable collections So why has he got two blonde hairs on his jacket?

But one strand of hair is a hair (countable).

His hair is black.

Hair is normally uncountable in English.

Is the spaghetti ready? These grapes are sour.

Fruit is very expensive, but vegetables are cheap.

Countable: vegetable(s), bean(s), pea(s), grape(s), lentil(s), pebble(s).

corn, wheat, barley, rye, maize, gravel.

Uncountable: fruit, rice, spaghetti, macaroni (and other pasta foods), sugar, salt, languages.

(e.g. grapes) may be countable in English and uncountable in certain other systematic: compare gravel and pebbles. Note that some words of this kind things (e.g. peas, grapes) are more often countable. But the difference is not very (e.g. sand, rice) are usually uncountable; collections made of larger grain-like while others are countable (usually plural). Things that come in small grains elements or as a mass. Some names for things of this kind are uncountable, Many things (e.g. rice, grapes) can be seen either as a collection of separate 5 fruit,     rice,    wheat,       spaghetti,          hair,     vegetables,            peas,     grapes Could I have two coffees? (= cups of coffee) Have you got any coffee?

The  same thing happens when we  talk about ordering drinks. Compare: We have a selection of fine wines at very good prices.

Not all washing powders are kind to your hands.

about different types.

Nouns for materials, liquids, etc can be countable when they are used to talk Would you like a glass of water?

- The window's made of unbreakable glass.

I'm going out to buy a paper (= a newspaper) - I'd like some typing paper.

countable noun to refer to something made of the material. Compare: Words for materials are uncountable, but we  can often use the same word as a 4 materials:      glass,      paper,       etc ‘ training session/course’  and ‘ planning operation/meeting' respectively).

The  same has happened with training and with parking: in English it means the activity of parking in general; French uses other languages and turned into countable nouns.
